PROJECT_CONTEXT.md.purpose: High-level repo map and durable project context, including the canonical market catalog and repository boundary. Use docs/app_architecture.md for canonical app code-organization policy.
read_when: Starting broad repo work
Explaining app architecture
Verifying setup or product context
skip_when: A narrow file-level pass already has scope and registry context

README.md.purpose: Human setup and runbook entry point.
read_when: Running the app
Checking environment commands
Helping the user self-serve
skip_when: Only source refactoring is needed

AGENTS.md.purpose: Short AI-agent entrypoint for source-of-truth routing, required starting loop, React surface governance, completion standards, and parent-led parallel worktree delegation.
read_when: Starting AI-assisted repo work
Choosing the required workflow loop
Using parallel agents or subagents
skip_when: A narrower context pack already identified the exact owner docs

docs/agent_operating_model.md.purpose: Canonical agent operating model for execution modes, broad cleanup contracts, reference-pattern rollouts, enforcement-integrity metadata, parent-led parallel worktree delegation, regression ledger policy, skill freshness, and workflow-quality measurement.
read_when: Starting broad cleanup, refactor, docs consolidation, design implementation, or workflow-harness changes
Using subagents, disposable Git worktrees, or parallel branch proposals
Updating workflow-quality metrics or project-local agent skills
Changing rule/tool enforcement mappings, vacuity proof metadata, or ratchet baselines
Changing runtime-checked tool role requirements or allowedFindings baseline receipt validation
skip_when: A trivial one-off command does not touch repo workflow

docs/ai_first_workflow_guide.md.purpose: Shareable companion guide explaining how Catch's agent entrypoint, context packs, docs registry, rules, scanners, lints, generated registries, audit receipts, CI gates, and readiness metrics combine into an AI-first workflow.
read_when: Explaining the Catch AI-first workflow to another repo owner
Implementing a similar agent workflow pattern in a different repository
Looking for a high-level map before opening the canonical owner docs
skip_when: Making Catch-specific rule, scanner, architecture, release, design, or code changes; use the owner docs selected by the context pack instead

docs/agent_regression_ledger.json.purpose: Machine-readable list of hard-won agent regression guards, including feature-contract projection grouping, exact route versus modal ownership, identity and enrichment fallback classification, actual CTA outcome naming, external-effect result handling, action wiring versus repository capability, unreachable-state classification, pending-mutation snapshot and cross-domain concurrency drift, audit-loop, design-system, generated-metadata, Widgetbook, canonical market display, nullable organizer data, parallel Flutter test, parallel delegation ownership, and enforcement-integrity drift failures.
read_when: Generating a context pack
Touching a scope with known agent regression risks
Adding a new recurring failure mode
skip_when: No repo files or recurring workflows are touched

docs/app_architecture.md.purpose: Canonical Flutter app architecture spec for dependency-direction seams, generated Riverpod provider topology and reviewed relationship candidates, Firebase/domain boundaries, mutation-cardinality grain, pending request snapshot integrity, app-role boundaries, feature/layer/screen/controller/repository/widget ownership, exhaustive screen-chrome role contracts, async state boundaries, error-surface taxonomy, logging test seams, coverage and test-spec maintainability, UI layout/scroll/sizing, routing/navigation constants, realtime stream lifecycle, reference implementation exhibits, exhibit-freshness markers, and enforcement policy.
read_when: Changing code organization under lib/
Starting or continuing an architecture migration rollout
Updating reference implementation exhibits
Classifying architecture pattern adopters, variants, or exceptions
Defining or reviewing feature folder structure
Changing host/consumer app-role boundaries or role-specific routing
Moving behavior between widgets, controllers, repositories, services, or domain classes
Deciding screen vs body/widget ownership
Changing async loading/error/mutation state boundaries
Choosing frozen, versioned, or independently keyed behavior for pending operations
Changing shared screen padding, tab body insets, sliver body gaps, scroll ownership, or sizing constraints
Changing exceptions, error mappers, operation context, logging, analytics, or user-facing error rendering
Changing keepAlive, autoDispose, or retained-tab stream ownership
Adding architecture lints, scanners, or overrides
Changing route literal scanners, frontend error scanner ratchets, or rule enforcement metadata for Flutter app architecture
Changing dependency-direction controller-seam carve-outs, Firebase/domain target policy, mutation-cardinality grain, or architecture exhibit freshness checks
Changing Flutter coverage reporting, expected-error test logging, or test-spec maintainability enforcement

Only changing generated widget inventory already covered by widget_catalog.md

Changing a callable or repository mutation
Auditing whether a UI supports zero, one, or many records
Deciding whether a product limit is real or just a UI shortcut
